sources.full: Descendants of the Rev. John Russell of Cambridge, Wethersfield, and Hadley
John Russell (son of Mary Talcott): bapt. 23 Sep 1650 First Church Hartford; d. 20 Jan 1670 Hadley; named in John Talcott's 1659 will bequest.
Descendants of the Rev. John Russell of Cambridge, Wethersfield, and Hadley
John Russell (son of Mary Talcott): bapt. 23 Sep 1650 First Church Hartford; d. 20 Jan 1670 Hadley; named in John Talcott's 1659 will bequest.
Descendants of the Rev. John Russell of Cambridge, Wethersfield, and Hadley
Primary sketch of Rev. John Russell of Hadley: Harvard 1645, Wethersfield to Hadley, sheltering the regicides, 1693 estate inventory (incl. three enslaved people), Hopkins School fight.

notable.notable_blurb: Puritan minister; the Angel of Hadley

narrative_blocks.header: The minister who hid the king’s killers
narrative_blocks.body: For over a decade Russell secretly sheltered Edward Whalley and William Goffe — two of the judges who had condemned Charles I to death — beneath his Hadley parsonage while royal agents hunted them across the colonies. A single misstep meant death for him and his household. The historian George Sheldon later called him the “Guardian Angel of Hadley.”

narrative_blocks.header: An angel rallied Hadley, then vanished
narrative_blocks.body: During a King Philip’s War raid, an unknown gray-haired stranger appeared, took command of the panicked townspeople, drove off the attackers, and disappeared — leaving them convinced an angel had saved them. The deliverer was almost certainly the fugitive general Goffe, slipping from Russell’s house and back. The episode became New England’s enduring “Angel of Hadley” legend.

narrative_blocks.header: He led his flock out of Connecticut
narrative_blocks.body: When a bitter dispute over baptism and church membership split the Wethersfield congregation, Russell drew up a covenant and gathered the dissenting families. In 1659 they agreed to leave the jurisdiction of Connecticut for Massachusetts. They crossed the wilderness and founded the town of Hadley on a bend of the Connecticut River.

narrative_blocks.header: Bones found beneath the parsonage cellar
narrative_blocks.body: When Russell’s house was pulled down near the end of the eighteenth century, workers lifting a cellar slab uncovered large human remains. Local tradition held them to be one of the regicides, secretly buried in the minister’s own foundation. A tooth from the find was kept by a witness for decades afterward.

narrative_blocks.header: Three enslaved people in his estate
narrative_blocks.body: The inventory of Russell’s estate, taken at Hadley in January 1693, listed an enslaved man, woman, and child together valued at sixty pounds. They are recorded among the household goods, a stark line in the ledger of a celebrated minister.

cross_connections.display_label: led the dissenting party Russell joined toward Hadley

sources.full: Mary (Talcott) Russell: only daughter of the Worshipful John & Dorothy (Mott) Talcott; m. 28 Jun 1649; died ~1650 soon after confinement (no death record survives).

narrative_blocks.header: The Worshipful John Talcott's only daughter
narrative_blocks.body: Mary was the eldest child and only daughter of the magistrate John Talcott of Hartford. Born in England, she came with the family in the great migration. She grew up in one of the colony's leading households.

narrative_blocks.header: She married the future Angel of Hadley
narrative_blocks.body: In June 1649 Mary wed the young minister John Russell at Hartford. She bore him a son before her early death around 1658. Her husband would later become famous as the protector of the regicides.

burial.plot_notes: Buried in Hartford. Name inscribed on monument erected by citizens of Hartford to perpetuate memory of the founders of the Colony of Connecticut.

political_offices.title: Select Man (Selectman)
political_offices.notes: Chosen at general town meeting Feb 4, 1634 along with Haynes, Bradstreet, and four others

political_offices.title: Representative
political_offices.notes: One of Representatives for Newtown (Cambridge), May 14, 1634

emigration.notes: 123 passengers including 50 children; all arrived in good health after 12-week passage. Fellow passengers included William Wadsworth, Tobie Willet (possible relation to Mary Willet who married Rev. Samuel Hooker), James Olmstedd, and others of Hooker's Company.

hartford_land.notes: Nine parcels originally granted: dwelling house on east side of Main Street abutting Centinel Hill; seven acres east of Great River; five acres in North Meadow; forty-seven acres meadow/swamp in North Meadow; one acre in Little Meadow; thirty acres in Old Oxpasture; seventeen acres in Cow Pasture; eleven acres in Venturers Field; sixteen acres in Neck of Land. Also gained 25 additional parcels by acquisition.

will.bequests_summary: Dwelling house and lands to wife Dorothy for her natural life; then to son John. Wethersfield property to son Samuel. Grandchildren John Russell (£20), John Talcott, Samuel Talcott, Elizabeth Talcott (£5 each). £5 to Rev. Samuel Stone; £5 toward a Latin school at Hartford. Overseers: Richard Lord Sr. and son John.
will.gift_to_stone: Five pounds to 'my reverend and beloved teacher, Mr. Stone' — Rev. Samuel Stone, Hartford minister and colleague of Thomas Hooker

narrative_blocks.body: Among the 'Adventurers Party' of 25 men who explored the Hartford area October 1635, prior to Hooker's party departure May 1636

narrative_blocks.header: His carpenter Nicholas Clark built the first house
narrative_blocks.body: His carpenter Nicholas Clark built the first house in Hartford on the ground where the North Church (Dr. Bushnell's) later stood

narrative_blocks.header: Was styled 'The Worshipful Mr. John Talcott'
narrative_blocks.body: Was styled 'The Worshipful Mr. John Talcott' in colonial records

narrative_blocks.header: Mother's daughter Rachel married Hartford Founder John S…
narrative_blocks.body: His mother's daughter Rachel married Hartford Founder John Steele; her sister Sarah married Hartford Founder William Wadsworth; he was an uncle of Hartford Founder John Skinner

bio.notes: The only Talcott known to have emigrated to America. Co-founder of Hartford with Rev. Thomas Hooker.

parents.discrepancy_note: One source gives parents as 'John Mott and Alice Harington'; Talcott 1876 gives 'Mark Mott and Frances Gutter' with Mark being son of Thomas and Alice (Mead) Mott. The Talcott 1876 reconstruction preferred as more detailed.
parents.mother_research_notes: Probably Frances Gutter (wife of Mark Mott). Frances died Braintree, buried Feb 23, 1615. Mark Mott died Braintree, buried Dec 14, 1637.
parents.father_research_notes: Probably Mark Mott, son of Thomas and Alice (Mead) Mott of Braintree, Essex. Talcott 1876 says 'supposed to have been the daughter of Mark, son of Thomas and Alice (Mead) Mott.'
